raw_consumer_statement:
I am a small business owner who creates custom commissioned artwork under a signed, non-refundable contract. On XX/XX/XXXX, a client paid a {$2000.00} deposit via Amex for a multi-element painting. Months later, they filed a chargeback for " product not received, '' despite acknowledging consistent progress, requesting ongoing changes, and remaining in contact until XX/XX/XXXX. 
At the time of the chargeback, the artwork was in active progress and no solidified delivery or completion date had ever been contractually promised or missed. On XX/XX/XXXX, I had submitted over 130 hours ' worth of documentation through XXXX, including the signed contract, annotated progress photos, calendar logs, and message history confirming the client 's approval and engagement. 
According to both XXXX 's and American Express ' chargeback policies, a non-receipt dispute is invalid if the work was not yet due. This service was evidently still in progress, with no violated delivery terms. This fact was thoroughly documented in the evidence I had submitted. 
Despite this, the chargeback was ruled in the client 's favor on XX/XX/XXXXXXXX  with no explanation, and no indication that the submitted materials were reviewed. XXXX  has since stated that they can not escalate this, a statement I've confirmed is inaccurate and inconsistent with standard merchant rights. 
I believe American Express failed to apply its own dispute standards and allowed chargeback misuse against a legitimate, custom, in-progress service. This has caused severe financial harm to my business.
